items.title: Click-through rate on ad logs
items.scenario: An ads team has 20M impression rows: user demographics, device, hour, past clicks, and label clicked (0/1). Features are numeric and high-cardinality categoricals with 5–15% missing device fields.
items.explanation: LightGBM with histogram bins and native categorical handling trains in minutes; leaf-wise growth captures rare interaction patterns. Compare validation AUC against a Random Forest baseline before deploying.

items.title: Insurance claim fraud scoring
items.scenario: Claims include amount, provider history, time since policy start, and missing repair estimates on 30% of rows. Regulators require feature importance summaries.
items.explanation: XGBoost handles NaN in-tree and exposes gain-based importance. Histogram training (`tree_method='hist'`) scales to millions of claims; early stopping on a temporal validation split avoids overfitting recent fraud patterns.

items.title: Kaggle tabular competition baseline
items.scenario: A team enters a structured-data competition with mixed numeric/categorical columns and a leaderboard driven by RMSE.
items.explanation: Standard playbook: LightGBM + XGBoost with 5-fold CV, log validation scores per round, ensemble top configs. Histogram splits make CV feasible within time limits — the libraries exist precisely for this workload.

items.title: Churn prediction for a telecom
items.scenario: Monthly snapshots per subscriber: tenure, plan price, support tickets, data usage — moderate size (500k rows), class imbalance (~8% churn).
items.explanation: Start with `scale_pos_weight` (XGBoost) or `is_unbalance` (LightGBM), shallow trees (`max_depth=6` or `num_leaves=31`), and track PR-AUC on validation — not accuracy alone.

items.title: House price regression with sparse features
items.scenario: Listing data: square footage, year built, zip, and many one-hot amenity columns (pool, garage) — mostly zeros.
items.explanation: XGBoost's sparsity-aware splits skip dense work on zero blocks. Histogram binning on continuous sq ft keeps split search fast; compare RMSE to linear regression baseline from **Linear Regression**.
